questions.explanationHtml: Python provides augmented assignment operators as a shorthand. The += operator adds the right-hand value to the left-hand variable and assigns the result back to the variable. Therefore, score += 1 is equivalent to score = score + 1.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following code block, which statements are true?

score = 0
points = 5
score = score + points
score = score + 1

choices.html: The code will result in an error because you cannot add two different variables together.
The line score = score + points updates the value of the score variable.

questions.explanationHtml: The variable score is initialized to 0. Then points (which is 5) is added, making score equal to 5. Finally, 1 is added, making the final value of score equal to 6. The score = score + points line is an expression that updates the value of an existing variable.

questions.promptHtml: Given a class Car with an attribute color, how would you create an object of this class named my_car and set its color to blue during creation?
questions.explanationHtml: To create an object (an instance) of a class, you call the class name as if it were a function, passing any required arguments to its initializer (__init__ method). The created object is then typically assigned to a variable.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet. What is the expected output?

class Student:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

student1 = Student("Maria")
print(student1.name)

student1.name = "Ana"
print(student1.name)

choices.html: The code will raise an error because attributes cannot be changed after initialization.

questions.explanationHtml: The code first creates an instance of the Student class and initializes its name attribute to "Maria", which is printed. Then, it modifies the name attribute of the same instance to "Ana" and prints the new value.

questions.promptHtml: Which line of code correctly accesses and prints the title attribute of a book object?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, you use the dot notation (.) to access the attributes of an object. The syntax is object_name.attribute_name. The print() function is then used to display the value of that attribute.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following class definition, which statements about the code are correct?

class Person:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

me = Person("Maria")
me.name = "Ana"
you = me
you.name = "Leo"

choices.html: After the code runs, the value of me.name will be Leo.
The variables me and you reference the same object in memory.
The line you = me creates a new Person object that is a copy of me.
The line me.name = "Ana" creates a new instance variable called name.

questions.explanationHtml: me and you are two variables pointing to the same Person object in memory. Initially, the object's name is set to 'Maria', then changed to 'Ana'. When you = me is executed, you now references the same object. Any change made through you (like you.name = 'Leo') will be reflected when accessing the object through me, because they are the same object.

questions.promptHtml: In object-oriented programming, what are the properties of an object often represented by?
questions.explanationHtml: Properties of an object, like the make or color of a car, are stored in variables (also known as attributes) within the object.

questions.promptHtml: If you were to model a 'Dog' as a Python object, which of the following would be best suited as a method?
questions.explanationHtml: Methods represent actions an object can perform. 'Bark' is an action, whereas 'breed', 'age', and 'name' are properties or characteristics of the dog.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary role of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To initialize the properties (attributes) of a new object.
To define the main action the object can perform.
To delete the object from memory when it's no longer needed.
To return a string representation of the object.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new object is created. Its main purpose is to initialize the object's properties (attributes).

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following Python class for a Car. Which of the following statements about this code are correct?

class Car:
    def __init__(self, make, model, color):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.color = color
        self.is_running = False

    def start_engine(self):
        self.is_running = True
        print(f"The {self.color} {self.model}'s engine is now running.")

my_car = Car("Toyota", "Corolla", "Red")

choices.html: my_car is an object of the Car class.
make and color are properties of the my_car object.
start_engine is a method of the Car class.
Car is an object, and my_car is its property.

questions.explanationHtml: my_car is an object (or instance) of the Car class. make and color are instance variables, initialized with values, representing properties of my_car. start_engine is a method that defines a behavior. Car is the class, not the object itself.

questions.promptHtml: Which method is used to add an element to the very end of a list?
questions.explanationHtml: The append() method is used to add a single element to the end of an existing list, modifying the list in-place.

questions.promptHtml:
What is the output of the following code snippet?

my_list = [10, 20, 30, 20, 40]
my_list.remove(20)
print(my_list)
questions.explanationHtml: The remove() method finds and removes only the first occurrence of the specified value. In this case, the first 20 at index 1 is removed, and the second 20 at index 3 (originally index 4) remains in the list.

questions.promptHtml:
After executing the code below, what will be the value of list_b?

list_a = [3, 1, 4]
list_b = list_a.sort()
questions.explanationHtml: The sort() method modifies the list in-place and returns None. Therefore, list_a becomes [1, 3, 4], but list_b is assigned the return value of the method, which is None.

questions.promptHtml: Given the list my_cards = ['A', 'K', 'Q', 'J'], which of the following lines of code will result in a ValueError?
questions.explanationHtml: A ValueError is raised by list.remove(x) when x is not present in the list. The values 'A' and 'Q' are in the list, so removing them works. However, '10' and 'a' (lowercase) are not in the list, so attempting to remove them will cause a ValueError.

questions.promptHtml: What is the purpose of the def __init__(self, ...) method inside a Python class?

choices.html: To define the main logic of the class.
To delete an instance of the class.
To initialize a new object's attributes when it is created.
To be called manually after an object is created.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method in Python classes, known as the constructor. It is automatically called when a new instance (object) of the class is created, and it is used to initialize the object's attributes.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following class structure. What is the correct way to call the attack method and print the result?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.health = 100

    def attack(self, enemy):
        damage = 20
        enemy.health -= damage
        print(f"{self.name} attacks {enemy.name}!")

player1 = Player("Hero")
player2 = Player("Goblin")
questions.explanationHtml: To call an instance method, you use the object.method(arguments) syntax. In this case, player1 is the object calling the attack method, and player2 is the enemy argument being passed to it. The self parameter is passed implicitly.

questions.promptHtml:
In the code snippet below, two Player objects are created. What will be the output of the final print statement?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.health = 100

    def attack(self, enemy):
        damage = 20
        enemy.health -= damage

hero = Player("Knight")
enemy = Player("Orc")
hero.attack(enemy)

print(enemy.health)
questions.explanationHtml: The hero.attack(enemy) call modifies the health attribute of the enemy object. The enemy object starts with health = 100. The attack method subtracts damage (20) from it. Therefore, enemy.health becomes 100 - 20, which is 80.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the Player class, which of the following statements about the attack method and its usage are true?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def attack(self, enemy):
        damage = 20
        enemy.health -= damage
        # Assume enemy object has name and health attributes

choices.html: The self parameter refers to the instance of the Player class that is performing the attack.
The enemy parameter is expected to be an object that has a health attribute.
The damage variable has a local scope and is not accessible outside the attack method.
When player1.attack(player2) is called, the method modifies an attribute of player1.

questions.explanationHtml: self refers to the instance calling the method (the attacker). enemy is a separate object passed as an argument (the target). The method directly modifies the health attribute of the enemy object. damage is a local variable; it only exists within the attack method's scope and is discarded after the method finishes.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used to handle a condition when the initial if statement is false, but you want to check for another specific condition?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, elif (short for else if) is used to check for an additional condition if the preceding if or elif conditions are not met. else is used as a catch-all when no conditions are true.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet. What will be the output if user_stat is 50 and bot_stat is 100?

if user_stat > bot_stat:
    print("You win!")
else:
    print("Computer wins.")
questions.explanationHtml: The code checks if user_stat (50) is greater than bot_stat (100). Since 50 is not greater than 100, the condition is false. The code inside the else block is executed, printing "Computer wins.".

questions.promptHtml:
Given the code below, what will be printed if choice is 'weight', user.weight is 85, and bot.weight is 70?

if choice == "speed":
    user_stat = user.speed
    bot_stat = bot.speed
elif choice == "weight":
    user_stat = user.weight
    bot_stat = bot.weight

if user_stat > bot_stat:
    print("You win!")
else:
    print("Computer wins.")

choices.html: The program will raise a NameError because user_stat is not defined.

questions.explanationHtml: The elif condition choice == "weight" is true, so user_stat becomes 85 and bot_stat becomes 70. Then, the second if statement checks if 85 > 70. This is true, so "You win!" is printed.

questions.promptHtml:
A programmer wants to expand the provided code to also handle a 'height' comparison. Which of the following code blocks, if inserted into the program, would correctly implement this feature? Assume the second if-else block for comparing stats remains the same.

# Original code block
if choice == "speed":
    user_stat = user.speed
    bot_stat = bot.speed
elif choice == "weight":
    user_stat = user.weight
    bot_stat = bot.weight
# Where new code would be inserted

# Comparison block
if user_stat > bot_stat:
    # ...

choices.html:
Replace the first elif with this code:

elif choice == "weight":
    user_stat = user.weight
    bot_stat = bot.weight
elif choice == "height":
    user_stat = user.height
    bot_stat = bot.height

Add this code after the existing elif block:

if choice == "height":
    user_stat = user.height
    bot_stat = bot.height

Replace the first elif block with a nested structure:

else:
    if choice == "weight":
        user_stat = user.weight
        bot_stat = bot.weight
    elif choice == "height":
        user_stat = user.height
        bot_stat = bot.height

Add this code after the existing elif block:

else choice == "height":
    user_stat = user.height
    bot_stat = bot.height

questions.explanationHtml: To add a new comparison for 'height', you can either add another elif block for choice == "height" or nest another if statement within a new else block. Both approaches correctly assign user.height and bot.height to the user_stat and bot_stat variables before the final comparison.

questions.promptHtml: Given the code bot = Player("Azula"), what does bot represent?
questions.explanationHtml: The line bot = Player("Azula") creates an instance, or object, of the Player class. The variable bot is used to store and refer to this specific object.

questions.promptHtml:
Based on the code below, how would you call the status() method for the second player, whose name is "Azula"?

me = Player("Katara")
bot = Player("Azula")
questions.explanationHtml: To call a method on a specific object, you use the syntax object_variable.method_name(). The second player object is stored in the variable bot, so the correct way to call its status method is bot.status().

questions.promptHtml: The code me = Player("Katara") creates an instance of the Player class. What is the process of creating an object from a class called?
questions.explanationHtml: Instantiation is the process of creating a concrete instance (an object) of a class. The __init__ method is the constructor that is called during this process.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet:

me = Player("Katara")
bot = Player("Azula")

Assuming Player is a class, which of the following statements are true?

choices.html: me and bot are two separate instances of the Player class.
The expression me is bot would evaluate to True.
If the Player class has a name attribute, me.name and bot.name would hold different string values.

questions.explanationHtml: me and bot are distinct objects, so me is bot is False. They are both created from the Player class, making them instances of Player. They are of the same type, Player. Each instance likely has its own name attribute initialized by the constructor, e.g., me.name would be 'Katara' and bot.name would be 'Azula'.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used to define a new class in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, the class keyword is used to create a new class, which serves as a blueprint for creating objects. The other keywords def, object, and new have different purposes.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following code snippets correctly creates an object (instance) of a Superhero class?
questions.explanationHtml: To create an object (or instance) of a class, you call the class name as if it were a function, like Superhero(). The result is a new object of that class, which is typically assigned to a variable.

questions.promptHtml: Which module and function would be most suitable for randomly selecting a card from a list of card objects named cards?
questions.explanationHtml: The random module provides functions for making random selections. The random.choice() function is specifically designed to pick one random item from a non-empty sequence, like a list.

questions.promptHtml: You are building a card game. You've created a Card class and a list of Card objects. Which of the following statements about this process are correct?

choices.html: The class is a blueprint for creating individual card objects.
Each Card object created from the class is a unique instance.
You can store multiple card objects in a Python list to manage them.
A class is an instance of an object.

questions.explanationHtml: class Card: correctly defines a class. card1 = Card() is the correct syntax for instantiation. Storing these instances in a list like [card1, card2] is a common and effective way to manage multiple objects. An instance is an object created from a class, not the other way around.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used to define a new class in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: The class keyword is used to create a new class definition in Python, which serves as a blueprint for creating objects.

questions.promptHtml: How do you correctly define a child class Sedan that inherits from a parent class Car?

choices.html:
class Sedan(Car):
    pass

class Sedan inherits Car:
    pass

class Sedan:
    inherit Car

class Sedan:
    parent = Car

questions.explanationHtml: In Python, inheritance is specified by placing the name of the parent class (or classes) in parentheses after the child class name in the class definition.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To destroy an object when it is no longer needed.
To initialize the attributes of a newly created object.
To return a string representation of the object.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new instance (object) of a class is created. Its main role is to initialize the attributes of the object.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code. Which of the following statements are true?

class Vehicle:
    def __init__(self, brand):
        self.brand = brand

    def honk(self):
        return "Beep!"

class Car(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, brand, model):
        super().__init__(brand)
        self.model = model

    def honk(self):
        return "Honk! Honk!"

my_car = Car("Toyota", "Corolla")

choices.html: Creating my_car will result in an error because brand is not defined in the Car class.

questions.explanationHtml: Car is a subclass of Vehicle. The isinstance() function confirms this relationship. The Car class overrides the honk method from its parent Vehicle, so calling my_car.honk() will execute the method defined in Car. The super().__init__(brand) call correctly initializes the brand attribute from the parent class.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following lines of code correctly creates an object, or an instance, of a class named Car?
questions.explanationHtml: To create an object (instance) of a class, you call the class name as if it were a function, followed by parentheses (), and assign the result to a variable. The syntax is variable_name = ClassName().

questions.promptHtml: Assume a class Animal is defined with an __init__ method that takes a name, weight, and speed. Which of the following correctly creates an instance of this class?
questions.explanationHtml: When instantiating a class, you must provide arguments that match the parameters defined in the __init__ method. The code lion = Animal("lion", 400, 32) correctly passes a string and two integers as required.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following code, what does the variable cheetah represent?

class Animal:
  def __init__(self, name, weight, speed):
    self.name = name
    self.weight = weight
    self.speed = speed

cheetah = Animal("cheetah", 130, 70)
questions.explanationHtml: The line cheetah = Animal(...) calls the Animal class constructor to create a new object. This object, an instance of the Animal class, is then assigned to the variable cheetah.

questions.promptHtml:
Which of the following statements about the code below are correct?

lion = Animal("lion", 400, 32, 12)
cheetah = Animal("cheetah", 130, 70, 14)

choices.html: lion is a variable that refers to an object of the Animal class.
Animal("cheetah", 130, 70, 14) is the constructor call used to create the cheetah object.
lion and cheetah refer to the same object in memory.
The code defines a new class called lion.

questions.explanationHtml: lion and cheetah are variables that hold two distinct objects created from the Animal class. The values "lion", 400, 32, 12 are arguments passed to the class's constructor (__init__ method) to initialize the state of the lion object.

questions.promptHtml: Which line of code correctly creates a list of animal names and assigns it to a variable named cards?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, a list is created using square brackets [], and string values must be enclosed in quotes (e.g., 'lion'). The assignment operator = is used to assign the list to the variable cards.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following list, how would you access and print the second element, 'gorilla'?

cards = ['lion', 'gorilla', 'whale']
questions.explanationHtml: Python lists use zero-based indexing, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. Therefore, cards[1] retrieves the second element.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary role of the parentheses () in the line cards = get_animal_list()?

choices.html: They execute or call the function named get_animal_list.

questions.explanationHtml: In Python, parentheses () after a name are used to call a function. This executes the code within the function and returns a value, which is then assigned to the cards variable.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet where lion, gorilla, and whale are predefined objects of the same class. Which of the following statements about the cards variable are true?

cards = [lion, gorilla, whale]

choices.html: cards is a list containing three object instances.
The cards variable is a tuple and cannot be modified after creation.

questions.explanationHtml: cards is a list, a mutable, ordered collection. Lists can hold any type of object, including custom class instances. cards[0] accesses the first element, which is the lion object, and len(cards) correctly returns the number of items in the list, which is 3.

questions.promptHtml: Which operator is used to check if two values are equal in an if statement?
questions.explanationHtml: The == operator is the equality operator in Python. It compares two values and returns True if they are equal, and False otherwise. The single = is the assignment operator.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of an if-else statement in Python?

choices.html: To execute different blocks of code based on a condition.
To repeat a block of code multiple times.
To store a value in a variable.

questions.explanationHtml: The if-else structure allows a program to execute one block of code if a certain condition is true, and a different block of code if the condition is false, enabling decision-making.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following code, what will be printed if score is 85?

score = 85
if score >= 90:
    print("A")
elif score >= 80:
    print("B")
else:
    print("C")
questions.explanationHtml: The code first checks if score >= 90. Since 85 is not greater than or equal to 90, this condition is false. It then moves to the elif score >= 80 condition. Since 85 is greater than or equal to 80, this condition is true, and the program prints 'B' and exits the conditional block.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet:

# Assume 'user' and 'bot' are objects with 'speed' and 'power' attributes.
user_speed = 100
bot_speed = 90
user_power = 80
bot_power = 120

category = "speed"

if category == "speed":
    if user_speed > bot_speed:
        result = "User wins!"
    else:
        result = "Bot wins!"
elif category == "power":
    if user_power > bot_power:
        result = "User wins!"
    else:
        result = "Bot wins!"

# What is the value of 'result' at this point?

Which of the following statements about this code are correct?

choices.html: The final value of the result variable will be "User wins!".
The code uses nested if-else statements to determine the winner within a category.
This code will raise a syntax error because if statements cannot be placed inside another if statement.

questions.explanationHtml: The variable category is 'speed', so the first if block is executed. Inside this block, user_speed (100) is greater than bot_speed (90), so the nested if condition is true. This assigns 'User wins!' to result. The elif block for 'power' is never checked because the initial if condition was met. The use of nested if statements is a valid way to handle sub-conditions.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following is an example of an integer in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, integers are whole numbers, positive or negative, without decimals. '-20' is a whole number.

questions.promptHtml: What is the data type of the following Python object? {'Jack': 12, 'Maria': 14}
questions.explanationHtml: This object is a dictionary, which stores data in key-value pairs. The curly braces {} are characteristic of dictionaries and sets, but the key: value format specifically defines a dictionary.

questions.promptHtml: What is the output of the following code? print(type([8, 9, 10, 12]))
questions.explanationHtml: The type() function returns the class type of an object. The object [8, 9, 10, 12] is a list, created using square brackets. Therefore, type() will identify it as <class 'list'>.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following are considered built-in collection data types in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: Lists and Dictionaries are fundamental, built-in collection types used to store multiple items. Integers are a numeric type, not a collection. While Turtle is a module available in Python's standard library, it is not a built-in data type like lists or dictionaries.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To initialize the attributes of an object when it is created.
To delete an object when it's no longer needed.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is the constructor for a class. It is called automatically when a new object of the class is created, and it is used to initialize the object's attributes.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the Person class, what will be the output of the code below?

class Person():
  def __init__(self, name, age):
    self.name = name
    self.age = age

person1 = Person("Alex", 25)
print(person1.name)
questions.explanationHtml: The code creates an instance of the Person class named person1. The __init__ method is called with "Alex" and 25, setting self.name to "Alex". The print statement then accesses and prints the name attribute of the person1 object.

questions.promptHtml:
Examine the following code. Why is self used as the first parameter in the __init__ method?

class Person():
  def __init__(self, name, age):
    self.name = name
    self.age = age

choices.html: self is a keyword that makes the method accessible from outside the class.
It refers to the instance of the object being created, allowing attributes like name and age to be assigned to it.
It is a placeholder for the class name, Person.
It's an optional parameter that can be omitted without changing the code's behavior.

questions.explanationHtml: In Python class methods, self is a conventional name for the first parameter, which refers to the instance of the class itself. It allows you to access and modify the attributes and methods of that specific object.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the Person class definition. Which of the following statements about its attributes are true?

class Person():
  def __init__(self, name, age):
    self.name = name
    self.age = age

choices.html: name and age are class variables, shared by all instances of Person.
Each instance of the Person class will have its own name and age attributes.
You can access the age attribute using Person.age without creating an instance.

questions.explanationHtml: name and age are instance variables, meaning each object of the Person class will have its own copy of these variables. They are created inside the __init__ method, which is the class constructor. They are accessed through an instance, like me.name, not directly through the class like Person.name.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following methods is used to add an element to the end of a list?
questions.explanationHtml: The append() method is used to add a single element to the very end of a list, modifying the list in place.

questions.promptHtml:
What is the output of the following Python code?

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 2]
print(numbers.count(2))
questions.explanationHtml: The count() method returns the number of occurrences of a specified element in a list. In this case, the number 2 appears 3 times in the list numbers.

questions.promptHtml:
After executing the following code, what will be the value of list_b?

list_a = [10, 20, 30]
list_b = list_a.copy()
list_a.clear()
questions.explanationHtml: The copy() method creates a shallow copy of the list. This means list_b becomes a new, independent list with the same elements as list_a at that moment. The subsequent clear() operation on list_a removes all elements from list_a but does not affect list_b because it is a separate object.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about list methods are true?

choices.html: Both list() and [] can be used to create an empty list.
After b = a.copy(), the expression a is b will evaluate to False.
The append() method modifies the list it is called on and returns None.
The count() method removes all occurrences of an element from a list.

questions.explanationHtml: list() and [] are both valid ways to create an empty list. The copy() method creates a new list object that is a shallow copy of the original; therefore, the original and the copy will have different memory addresses. append() modifies the list in-place and returns None.

questions.promptHtml: Which line of code must be written at the beginning of a Python file to use functions like randint()?
questions.explanationHtml: The import random statement makes the random module and its functions, such as randint(), available for use in the program.

questions.promptHtml: Given the code damage = random.randint(10, 50), what is the highest possible value that the damage variable can hold?
questions.explanationHtml: The random.randint(a, b) function returns a random integer N such that a <= N <= b. Therefore, the range is inclusive of both endpoints.

questions.promptHtml:
The code snippet below is part of a game where a character's health is reduced. What does the line enemy.health -= damage do?

# enemy.health is 100
# damage is 30
enemy.health -= damage

choices.html: It assigns the negative value of damage to enemy.health.
It subtracts the value of damage from enemy.health and updates enemy.health with the new value.
It compares enemy.health and damage and returns a boolean value.

questions.explanationHtml: The -= operator is a shorthand assignment operator. x -= y is equivalent to x = x - y. It subtracts the value on the right from the variable on the left and assigns the result back to that variable.

questions.promptHtml:
Based on the provided code snippet, which of the following statements are true?

import random

class Pikachu(Player):
    # ... (other class details)
    def shock(self, enemy):
        damage = random.randint(10, 50)
        enemy.health -= damage
        # ...

choices.html: shock is a method that belongs to the Pikachu class.
The Pikachu class is a subclass of the Player class.
The value of damage will always be less than 50.
The enemy object passed to the shock method is expected to have a health attribute.

questions.explanationHtml: shock is a method of the Pikachu class because it is defined inside the class block with self as its first parameter. Pikachu inherits from Player as indicated by class Pikachu(Player):. The random.randint(10, 50) function will generate an integer between 10 and 50, inclusive. The enemy parameter is expected to be an object with an attribute named health.

questions.promptHtml: In Python, everything is considered a(n) ________, including numbers, strings, and lists.
questions.explanationHtml: In Python's object-oriented paradigm, every piece of data and even functions are implemented as objects, each with its own type, identity, and value.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To destroy an object when it is no longer needed.
To initialize the attributes of a newly created object.
To define the string representation of an object.
To create a copy of an existing object.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new object of a class is created. Its main role is to initialize the attributes of the object.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet. What will be the output?

class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

dog1 = Dog("Buddy")
dog2 = dog1
dog2.name = "Lucy"

print(dog1.name)
questions.explanationHtml: When dog2 = dog1 is executed, both dog1 and dog2 variables point to the same Dog object in memory. Therefore, modifying the name attribute through dog2 also changes the name for dog1 because they reference the exact same object.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about objects in Python are true?

choices.html: Every object has a unique identity, which can be retrieved using the id() function.
The is operator compares the identity of two objects, not their values.
All objects in Python are mutable, meaning their state can be changed after creation.
A tuple is an example of an immutable object.

questions.explanationHtml: In Python, everything is an object. The id() function gives a unique integer for each object's memory address. The is operator checks if two variables refer to the same object (i.e., have the same id). Tuples are immutable objects, meaning their contents cannot be changed after creation, unlike lists which are mutable.

questions.promptHtml: Which line of Python code will pause the execution of a program for 3 seconds?

choices.html:
import time
time.sleep(3)

import time
time.wait(3)

import time
sleep(3)

questions.explanationHtml: The time.sleep() function is used to suspend the execution of the current thread for a given number of seconds. You must first import time to use it.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary function of the os module in Python, based on the command os.system("clear")?

choices.html: To interact with the operating system and execute system commands.

questions.explanationHtml: The os module provides a way to interact with the operating system. os.system() executes a command in a subshell, like typing it directly into the command line. clear is a common command-line utility for clearing the terminal screen.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet. What will be its behavior?

import time

print("Start")
time.sleep(2)
print("End")

choices.html: It will print "Start", wait for 2 seconds, and then print "End".
It will wait for 2 seconds, then print "Start" and "End" together.
It will raise an error because sleep is not a valid function.

questions.explanationHtml: The program first prints 'Start', then time.sleep(2) pauses the execution for 2 seconds. After the pause, the program resumes and prints 'End'.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about the os.system() and time.sleep() functions are correct?

choices.html: The command passed to os.system() might need to be changed to work on different operating systems (e.g., 'cls' on Windows vs. 'clear' on Linux).
time.sleep() can accept a floating-point number to pause for a fraction of a second.
Both the os and time modules must be installed using pip before they can be imported.
os.system("clear") will work on all operating systems without any changes.

questions.explanationHtml: os.system('clear') is specific to Unix-like systems (Linux, macOS); on Windows, the command is cls. The os.system() function's behavior is therefore platform-dependent. Both os and time are part of the Python standard library, so they do not require a separate installation via pip. time.sleep() accepts a float for sub-second precision.

questions.promptHtml: Which Python function is used to get input from a user, such as asking them to choose a move in a game?
questions.explanationHtml: The input() function is used to prompt the user for input and reads a line from the console, returning it as a string.

questions.promptHtml: To make a computer choose a random move from a list of options like ['attack', 'defend', 'heal'], which module and function would be most appropriate?
questions.explanationHtml: The random.choice() function is ideal for selecting a single item at random from a sequence, such as a list of available moves.

questions.promptHtml: Which type of loop is best suited to repeat a series of game steps (like taking turns) until a specific condition is met, such as a player's health reaching zero?

choices.html: A while loop that checks if the game is over.
A for loop that iterates through a list of players.
An if-elif-else statement to check the winner once.
A recursive function without a base case.

questions.explanationHtml: A while loop is perfect for repeating a block of code as long as a condition is true. It will continue the game turns until the winning condition is met, at which point the loop terminates.

questions.promptHtml: When creating players for a battle game, which of the following Python data structures could be effectively used to store player data, such as name, health, and attack power?

choices.html: A dictionary, where keys are attribute names ('name', 'health') and values are the player's stats.
A custom class (e.g., class Player:) with attributes for name, health, and attack power.
A set, because it ensures each player has unique stats.
A tuple, containing the player's name, health, and attack power in a fixed order.

questions.explanationHtml: A dictionary is great for storing key-value pairs like 'name': 'Hero'. A custom class is the best object-oriented approach, encapsulating data and behavior. A tuple could work for simple, immutable player data, but it is less descriptive than the other two options.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used to define a function in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: The def keyword is fundamental for defining a new function in Python, followed by the function name and parentheses.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To initialize the properties of an object when it is created.
To destroy an object when it is no longer needed.
To define the main logic of the class.
To return a string representation of the object.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new object (instance) of a class is created. Its main role is to initialize the object's attributes.

questions.promptHtml:
What will be the output of the following code snippet?

count = 5
while count > 1:
  print(count)
  count -= 1
  if count == 3:
    break
else:
  print("Loop finished")

choices.html:
5
4
3
2
Loop finished

questions.explanationHtml: The while loop starts with count as 5. It prints 5, then 4. When count becomes 3, the if condition is met and the break statement is executed, terminating the loop immediately. The else block associated with the while loop is only executed if the loop completes normally (i.e., its condition becomes false), not when it's terminated by a break.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following are valid ways to import the sqrt function from the math module so it can be called directly as sqrt()?

choices.html: from math import sqrt
import sqrt from math

questions.explanationHtml: from math import sqrt brings only the sqrt function into the current namespace. from math import * imports all public objects from the math module into the current namespace, which includes sqrt. The standard import math would require you to call the function using dot notation (math.sqrt()).

questions.promptHtml: In Python, what is a class?

choices.html: A specific instance of a data structure.
A type of variable that holds a single value.

questions.explanationHtml: A class is a blueprint or a template for creating objects. An object is an instance of a class, which has its own attributes and methods as defined by the class.

questions.promptHtml: What is the purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To delete an object when it is no longer needed.
To define the string representation of an object.
To initialize the attributes of an object when it is created.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, also known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new object of a class is created. It is used to initialize the object's attributes.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following Python code. What will be the output?

class Dog:
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

dog1 = Dog("Buddy")
dog2 = Dog("Lucy")

Dog.species = "Wolf"

print(f"{dog1.name}: {dog1.species}")
print(f"{dog2.name}: {dog2.species}")
questions.explanationHtml: species is a class variable shared by all instances of the Dog class. When Dog.species is changed, this change is reflected across all instances that haven't overridden it. Therefore, both dog1.species and dog2.species will print the new value, "Wolf".

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about classes and objects in Python are true?

choices.html: A class can inherit attributes and methods from another class.
The self parameter in an instance method refers to the instance of the class itself.
Instance attributes are shared by all objects of a class.
In Python, nearly everything, including built-in types like integers and strings, is an object.

questions.explanationHtml: Inheritance allows a new class (subclass) to inherit attributes and methods from an existing class (superclass). The self parameter refers to the specific instance of the class on which a method is called. Class attributes are shared among all instances, while instance attributes are unique to each object. Everything in Python is an object, including integers, strings, and functions.

questions.promptHtml: Which symbol is used to access an object's attributes and methods in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, the dot (.) is used to access the attributes (data) and methods (functions) belonging to an object.

questions.promptHtml: Given a Car class, how would you correctly set the color attribute of a my_car object to 'blue'?
questions.explanationHtml: To assign a value to an object's attribute, you use the object's name, followed by a dot, the attribute name, the assignment operator (=), and the value.

questions.promptHtml: What is the key difference between accessing an attribute like car.color and calling a method like car.start()?

choices.html: There is no difference; both are used to get data.
car.color retrieves a data value, whereas car.start() executes a function.
car.color can only be used for strings, but car.start() works with any data type.
Attributes are accessed with () and methods are not.

questions.explanationHtml: Accessing an attribute retrieves a data value, while calling a method (indicated by parentheses) executes a function associated with the object, performing an action.

questions.promptHtml: Assuming my_string is a string object, which of the following lines of code correctly use dot notation to call a method or access an attribute?
questions.explanationHtml: my_string.upper() is a valid method call that returns an uppercase version of the string. my_string.strip() is also a valid method call that removes leading/trailing whitespace. my_string.length is incorrect; the function len(my_string) is used instead. my_string.replace = 'a' is incorrect because you cannot reassign a built-in method like replace this way.

questions.promptHtml: Which Python keyword is used to start a loop that iterates over a sequence of elements?
questions.explanationHtml: The for keyword in Python is used to create a loop that iterates over a sequence (like a list, tuple, or string). The code for c in cards: initiates a loop that will execute its indented block for each item in the cards sequence.

questions.promptHtml: Given the code for c in cards: print(c.name), what can be inferred about the structure of the elements within the cards variable?

choices.html: Each element in cards is an object with a name attribute.
cards is a dictionary where each value has a name key.
cards is a list of lists, and name is an index.

questions.explanationHtml: The expression c.name uses dot notation to access an attribute named name from the object c. Since c represents each element from the cards list during the loop, it implies that cards is a collection of objects, and each object has a .name attribute.

questions.promptHtml:
Assume cards is a list where each element is an object with a .name attribute. Which of the following code snippets would produce the same output as the original code?

Original code:

print("Welcome to the game.")
print("Here are the cards:")
for c in cards:
    print(c.name)

choices.html:
print("Welcome to the game.\nHere are the cards:")
print(cards.name)

print("Welcome to the game.\nHere are the cards:")
names = (c.name for c in cards)
print(names)

print("Welcome to the game.")
print("Here are the cards:")
names = [c.name for c in cards]
print(*names, sep='\n')

print("Welcome to the game.")
print("Here are the cards:")
for name in cards.name:
    print(name)

questions.explanationHtml: The print() function can take multiple arguments, and by default, it separates them with a space. The sep='\n' argument changes the separator to a newline character. Using a list comprehension [c.name for c in cards] creates a list of names, and the * operator unpacks this list, passing each name as a separate argument to print().

questions.promptHtml: Based on the line for c in cards:, which of the following types could the cards variable be?
questions.explanationHtml: The for...in loop in Python can iterate over any iterable object. Lists (list), tuples (tuple), and sets (set) are all common iterable data structures that can be used in a for loop. An integer (int) is not iterable and would raise a TypeError.

questions.promptHtml: What is the correct syntax to define a class named Student in Python?

choices.html:
define class Student:
    pass

class Student()
    pass

class Student:
    pass

questions.explanationHtml: In Python, a class is defined using the class keyword, followed by the class name and a colon. The class body is indented.

questions.promptHtml: Suppose you have a file named shapes.py that contains a class Circle. Which is the correct way to import only the Circle class into your main.py file?
questions.explanationHtml: The from <module_name> import <class_name> syntax is used to import a specific class from a module, making it directly accessible in the current namespace.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of the __init__ method in a Python class?

choices.html: To destroy an object when it's no longer in use.
To initialize the attributes of a newly created object.
To define the main logic of the class.
To import other modules into the class.

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is a special method, known as a constructor, that is automatically called when a new instance (object) of a class is created. It is used to initialize the object's attributes.

questions.promptHtml: Given a person.py file with the Person class defined, what are the potential consequences of using from person import * in main.py?

choices.html: It can lead to naming conflicts if main.py defines a variable or function with the same name as one in person.py.
It can make the code harder to read and debug because the origin of imported names is not explicit.
It will cause a SyntaxError because this form of import is not allowed in Python.
All classes, functions, and variables from person.py (that don't start with an underscore) become available in main.py.

questions.explanationHtml: Using from module import * imports all public names from the module into the current namespace. This can lead to name clashes if the importing file already has a variable or function with the same name. It also makes the code less readable because it's unclear where a specific name originated from. While it can make the code shorter by avoiding module prefixes, this is generally discouraged in favor of clarity.

questions.promptHtml: Which line of code correctly imports the random module so you can use its functions?
questions.explanationHtml: The correct syntax to make the functions of a module available in your script is import module_name. In this case, to use functions like random.choice(), you must first import the random module.

questions.promptHtml: Given a list colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue'], which snippet of code correctly selects a random color and assigns it to a variable named chosen_color?

choices.html:
import random
chosen_color = random.choice(colors)

import random
chosen_color = random.select(colors)

import random
chosen_color = colors.random()

import random
chosen_color = choice(colors)

questions.explanationHtml: The random.choice() function takes a sequence (like a list) as an argument and returns a randomly selected element from it. This is the most direct way to select a random item from a list.

questions.promptHtml:
What is the purpose of the cards.remove(user) line in the following code snippet?

import random

cards = ['Ace', 'King', 'Queen', 'Jack']
user = random.choice(cards)
cards.remove(user)
bot = random.choice(cards)

choices.html: To ensure the bot cannot be assigned the same card that was assigned to the user.
To add the user's card to a different list.
To check if the user's card is present in the cards list.

questions.explanationHtml: After a card is chosen for the user, cards.remove(user) is used to remove that specific card from the cards list. This ensures that when the bot's card is chosen from the same list, it cannot be the same card that the user already has.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following code snippet:

import random
cards = ['A', 'B']
user = random.choice(cards)
cards.remove(user)
bot = random.choice(cards)

Which of the following statements are true about the final state of the cards list and the values of the user and bot variables?

choices.html: The user and bot variables will always hold different values.
The final length of the cards list will be 1.
It's possible for user and bot to have the same value.
The code will raise an error because random.choice cannot be called on a list with one element.

questions.explanationHtml: Initially, cards has two elements. random.choice(cards) assigns either 'A' or 'B' to user. Then cards.remove(user) removes that chosen element, leaving only one card in the list. random.choice(cards) is then called on a list with a single element, so bot must be assigned that remaining element. Therefore, user and bot will have different values ('A' and 'B', or 'B' and 'A'), and the cards list will end up with one element.

questions.promptHtml: In Object-Oriented Programming, what is a class that inherits attributes and methods from another class called?
questions.explanationHtml: A child class, also known as a derived class or subclass, is created based on an existing parent class (or base class), inheriting its properties and behaviors. This is a fundamental concept of inheritance.

questions.promptHtml: Which code snippet correctly demonstrates the basic syntax for a child class Wizard inheriting from a parent class Player?

choices.html:
class Wizard:
    inherit Player

class Wizard(inherits=Player):
    pass

class Wizard(Player):
    pass

class Player(Wizard):
    pass

questions.explanationHtml: The correct syntax for inheritance in Python is class ChildClassName(ParentClassName):. The parent class's name is placed in parentheses after the child class's name.

questions.promptHtml: Which built-in Python module is commonly used to make a random selection from a list of items, such as choosing a player character?
questions.explanationHtml: The random module provides functions for making random selections. Specifically, random.choice() is used to pick a random element from a non-empty sequence like a list.

questions.promptHtml: A Player parent class exists. Which of the following statements are true about a Wizard child class that inherits from it?

choices.html: The Wizard class can have its own new methods that the Player class does not have.
An object of the Wizard class can call methods defined in the Player class.
The Player class automatically gains access to new methods defined in the Wizard class.
The Wizard class's __init__ method can (and often should) call the Player class's __init__ method using super().

questions.explanationHtml: A child class inherits all public methods and attributes from its parent. It can also have its own unique methods. Using super().__init__() is the standard way to call the parent's constructor to ensure proper initialization of inherited attributes.

questions.promptHtml: In Python, what is a class often described as?

choices.html: A specific instance of a data structure.

questions.explanationHtml: A class acts as a template or blueprint from which individual objects (instances) are created. It defines a set of attributes and methods that the objects will have.

questions.promptHtml: What is the relationship between a class and an object in Python?

choices.html: An object is an instance of a class.
A class is an instance of an object.
A class and an object are the same thing.
An object is a blueprint for a class.

questions.explanationHtml: An object is an 'instance' of a class. The class defines the structure and behavior (attributes and methods), and the object is a concrete realization of that class with actual data.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following code snippets correctly defines a Car class and creates an object (instance) of it?

choices.html:
def Car():
  pass
my_car = Car

object Car:
  pass
my_car = new Car()

class Car:
  pass
my_car = Car()

class Car():
  pass
my_car = object(Car)

questions.explanationHtml: The class keyword is used to define a class, followed by the class name. The pass statement is a placeholder for future code. An object is created by calling the class name as if it were a function, like my_car = Car().

questions.promptHtml: Given the 'class is a blueprint' analogy, which of the following statements are true about Object-Oriented Programming in Python?

choices.html: You can create many different object instances from a single class.
Each object created from a class can have its own unique state (e.g., one Car object can be red, another can be blue).
An object is just another name for a class; they are fundamentally the same.
Once an object is created, changing its attributes will also change the class blueprint itself.

questions.explanationHtml: A single class (blueprint) can create multiple distinct objects (cars), each with its own state. The class defines the shared attributes (like number_of_wheels), but the specific attributes of each object (like color or mileage) are unique to that instance. The object itself is the concrete entity, not just a reference to the blueprint.

questions.promptHtml: How do you write a single-line comment in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, the hash symbol (#) is used to start a single-line comment. The interpreter ignores everything on the line that follows the #.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about Python's case-sensitivity is true?

choices.html: Python ignores case, so myVar and myvar are the same.
Python treats myVar and myvar as two different variables.
Keywords like if and for can be written in any case (e.g., IF or For).

questions.explanationHtml: Python is a case-sensitive language, meaning it distinguishes between uppercase and lowercase letters. Therefore, myVariable, MyVariable, and myvariable would be treated as three distinct variables.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary role of indentation in Python?

choices.html: To make the code look neat and organized, but it has no effect on execution.

questions.explanationHtml: Unlike many other languages that use braces {}, Python uses indentation to define blocks of code, such as the body of a function, loop, or conditional statement. Incorrect indentation will lead to an IndentationError.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about Python syntax are correct?

choices.html: The variables name and Name are treated as two separate variables.
A line starting with # is ignored by the Python interpreter.
Inconsistent indentation within the same code block will raise an error.
Python uses curly braces {} to define code blocks, similar to Java or C++.

questions.explanationHtml: Python is case-sensitive, so name and Name are different. The # symbol is used for single-line comments. Indentation is crucial for defining code blocks and must be consistent within a block; incorrect indentation causes a syntax error.

questions.promptHtml: What is the purpose of the getattr() function in Python?

choices.html: To set the value of an object's attribute.
To get the value of an object's attribute using its name as a string.
To check if an object has a certain attribute.
To create a new attribute for an object.

questions.explanationHtml: The getattr(object, 'attribute_name') function is used to dynamically get the value of an object's attribute using its name as a string, which is equivalent to object.attribute_name.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements about the getattr() function are true?

choices.html: If the specified attribute does not exist, getattr() will raise an AttributeError.
getattr() can take an optional third argument, which acts as a default value if the attribute is not found.
The attribute name passed to getattr() must be a literal string and cannot be a variable.

questions.explanationHtml: getattr() dynamically accesses an attribute using a string name. It raises an AttributeError if the attribute doesn't exist, unless a default value is provided as a third argument, in which case that default value is returned instead of an error.

questions.promptHtml:
In the context of object-oriented programming in Python, what does the following code snippet represent?

class Spiderman(Player):
    pass

choices.html: Creating a function called Spiderman that takes Player as an argument.
Defining a class named Spiderman that inherits from the Player class.
Creating an instance of the Player class named Spiderman.

questions.explanationHtml: The syntax class ChildName(ParentName): is used to define a new class ChildName that inherits from an existing class ParentName. In this case, Spiderman is the child class and Player is the parent class.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the Player parent class, which of the following correctly defines a child class Hero and calls the parent's __init__ method inside the child's __init__?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

choices.html:
class Hero(Player):
    def __init__(self):
        self.__init__("Hero")

class Hero(Player):
    def __init__(self):
        init(self, "Hero")

class Hero(Player):
    def __init__(self):
        Player.__init__(self, "Hero")

class Hero(Player):
    def __init__(self):
        self.Player.__init__("Hero")

questions.explanationHtml: To call the parent class's __init__ method, you explicitly reference the parent class by name (Player), call its __init__ method, and pass self as the first argument, followed by any other required arguments.

questions.promptHtml: What is the primary purpose of calling Player.__init__(self, "Spiderman") inside the __init__ method of the Spiderman child class?

choices.html: To create a new, separate instance of the Player class.
To initialize the part of the Spiderman object that is inherited from the Player class.
To check if the Spiderman class is a valid child of the Player class.
To override all methods from the Player class.

questions.explanationHtml: Calling the parent's __init__ method ensures that any initialization logic defined in the parent class (like setting attributes) is executed for instances of the child class. It's a fundamental part of extending functionality while reusing the parent's setup code.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following class definitions, which statements will execute without raising an error?

class Character:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

class Spiderman(Character):
    def __init__(self):
        Character.__init__(self, "Spiderman")
        self.power = "web-slinging"
questions.explanationHtml: Instantiating Spiderman() works because its __init__ calls the parent __init__ correctly. You can access attributes defined in both the child (power) and parent (name) initializers. The super() function is an alternative and often preferred way to call the parent method. Character() requires the 'name' argument, so calling it without one will raise a TypeError.

questions.promptHtml: Given a dictionary player_moves = {"punch": 10, "kick": 15}, which code snippet correctly iterates through and prints the keys of the dictionary?

choices.html:
for move in player_moves:
    print(move)

for move in player_moves:
    print(player_moves)
print(all moves in player_moves)

questions.explanationHtml: A standard for loop over a dictionary iterates through its keys. The variable move will be assigned each key ('punch', 'kick') in sequence, and print(move) will display it.

questions.promptHtml: Assume a Player class exists. What does the line me = Player("Katara") do in Python?

choices.html: It creates an instance of the Player class and assigns it to the variable me.
It defines a new class named me that inherits from the Player class.
It calls a function named Player with the argument "Katara" and stores the result in me.
It compares the variable me to a Player object named "Katara".

questions.explanationHtml: This line demonstrates object instantiation. It calls the Player class's constructor to create a new object (an instance of the Player class) and assigns this object to the variable me.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following class definition. What will be the output of the subsequent code?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.moves = {"water whip": 20, "ice wall": 30}

me = Player("Katara")
for m in me.moves:
    print(m)

choices.html:
("water whip", 20)
("ice wall", 30)

questions.explanationHtml: The code creates a Player object named me. The for loop then iterates over the me.moves dictionary. By default, iterating over a dictionary yields its keys. Therefore, the loop will print each key, 'water whip' and 'ice wall', on a new line.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the class below, which of the following code snippets are valid ways to display the moves of a Player instance?

class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.moves = {"air scooter": 10, "air blast": 25}
    
    def show_moves(self):
        for move_name in self.moves.keys():
            print(move_name)

choices.html:
a_player = Player("Aang")
for move in a_player.moves:
    print(move)

a_player = Player("Aang")
for move in a_player.moves.keys():
    print(move)

questions.explanationHtml: Calling the show_moves method is a direct way to use the class's functionality. Iterating directly over the moves dictionary (for move in a_player.moves:) loops through its keys, which is a common and valid pattern. Using a_player.moves.keys() explicitly in a loop also works and achieves the same result.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following lines of code correctly creates an instance of a class named Car?
questions.explanationHtml: To create an object (instance) of a class, you call the class name as if it were a function, passing any required arguments. The result is then typically assigned to a variable. my_car = Car() is the standard syntax for this process.

questions.promptHtml: Based on the line plane = Vehicle("Boeing 747", 8, 416), what would the __init__ method definition for the Vehicle class most likely look like?

choices.html:
def __init__(self):
    pass

def __init__(self, model, engines, capacity):
    pass

def __init__(model, engines, capacity):
    pass

def Vehicle(self, model, engines, capacity):
    pass

questions.explanationHtml: The __init__ method is the constructor for a class. The first parameter is always self, which refers to the instance being created. The subsequent parameters correspond to the arguments passed when the object is instantiated. In this case, "Boeing 747", 8, and 416 are passed, so the __init__ method needs self plus three other parameters to accept these values.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following code, what is the most likely purpose of the values 130, 70, and 14?

cheetah = Animal("cheetah", 130, 70, 14)

choices.html: They are arguments for a method that will be called later.
They are arguments used to initialize the attributes of the cheetah object.
They define the number of Animal objects to be created.

questions.explanationHtml: When an object is created, the values passed to the class constructor are used to initialize the object's attributes. These values become the initial state of the object, representing its characteristics, such as weight, speed, and age.

questions.promptHtml:
A programmer creates several Animal objects as shown below. Which of the following statements are likely true about the Animal class and its objects?

lion = Animal("lion", 400, 32, 12)
cheetah = Animal("cheetah", 130, 70, 14)
gorilla = Animal("gorilla", 450, 22, 50)

choices.html: Animal is a class, and lion is an instance of that class.
The Animal class's __init__ method likely accepts five parameters in total.
lion, cheetah, and gorilla all refer to the same object in memory.
gorilla is a variable holding an object of type Animal.

questions.explanationHtml: The code shows Animal being called with arguments to create instances, making it a class. lion, cheetah, and gorilla are separate instances, each a distinct object in memory. The __init__ method must accept self and four additional arguments corresponding to the values provided during instantiation.

questions.promptHtml: In object-oriented programming, what is a function that is defined inside a class called?
questions.explanationHtml: A function defined within a class is known as a method. It operates on the data contained within an object of that class.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the code below, what will be printed to the console when player1.status() is called?

class Player:
  def __init__(self, name):
    self.name = name
    self.health = 100

  def status(self):
    print(self.name, "health:", self.health)

player1 = Player("Zuko")
player1.status()
questions.explanationHtml: The status() method is called on the player1 object. Inside the method, self.name refers to player1.name ("Zuko") and self.health refers to player1.health (100). The print() function will display these values separated by spaces.

questions.promptHtml: What is the purpose of the self parameter in a class method in Python?

choices.html: It refers to the instance of the class, allowing access to its attributes and methods.
It is a special keyword that makes the method private and inaccessible from outside the class.
It is an optional parameter that holds global variables for the class.
It refers to the class itself, not the instance, for accessing class-level variables.

questions.explanationHtml: The self parameter is a conventional name for the first argument of a method in a class. It represents the instance of the class itself, allowing the method to access and modify the object's attributes and other methods.

questions.promptHtml:
Based on the provided code structure, which of the following statements are true about the Player class and its usage?

class Player:
  def __init__(self, name):
    self.name = name
    # ... health initialization is missing

  def status(self):
    print(self.name, "health:", self.health)

me = Player("Katara")
# me.status()

choices.html: The __init__ method is a special method called a constructor, which is automatically invoked when a new object of the class is created.
If me.status() were executed, it would raise an AttributeError because the health attribute was never initialized for the me object.
The line me = Player("Katara") will cause a TypeError because the __init__ method is incomplete.
In the status method, self refers to the me object instance.

questions.explanationHtml: __init__ is the constructor used to initialize a new object's state. Since self.health is never assigned a value in the __init__ method, calling me.status() would try to access an attribute that doesn't exist, raising an AttributeError. Creating an instance me = Player("Katara") is a valid operation that calls the __init__ method.

questions.promptHtml: If you want to use the turtle graphics library in your Python script, what is the first step you typically need to take?

choices.html: Use the import turtle statement at the beginning of the script.
Create a turtle object directly, like my_turtle = Turtle().
Download the turtle library using pip install in the script.

questions.explanationHtml: To use any external library or module in Python, like turtle, you must first import it using the import statement. This makes the module's functions and classes available for use in your code.

questions.promptHtml: How would you create three separate Turtle objects and store them in a list for later use?
questions.explanationHtml: A list is an ideal data structure for storing multiple objects. You can create a list and append new instances of the turtle.Turtle() class to it. While you can create them one by one, a list comprehension or a for loop is a more efficient and scalable way to create multiple objects.

questions.promptHtml: Given a list of turtle objects named turtles, what is the correct way to iterate through the list and make each turtle draw a circle?

choices.html:
for i in turtles:
    turtles.circle(50)

for t in range(len(turtles)):
    circle(50)

for t in turtles:
    t.circle(50)

questions.explanationHtml: A for loop is used to iterate over a sequence, such as a list. In each iteration, the loop variable (e.g., t) will hold one turtle object from the turtles list. You can then call methods like circle() on that specific object.

questions.promptHtml: You want to create five turtles, each with a different color from a list and at a different starting position. Which of the following code snippets correctly achieve this? (Assume turtle has been imported)

choices.html:
colors = ['red', 'blue', 'green', 'orange', 'purple']
turtles = []
for i, color in enumerate(colors):
    t = turtle.Turtle()
    t.color(color)
    t.penup()
    t.goto(i * 50, 0)
    t.pendown()
    turtles.append(t)

colors = ['red', 'blue', 'green', 'orange', 'purple']
turtles = []
for i in range(len(colors)):
    t = turtle.Turtle()
    t.color(colors[i])
    t.penup()
    t.setposition(i * 50, 0)
    t.pendown()
    turtles.append(t)

colors = ['red', 'blue', 'green', 'orange', 'purple']
t = turtle.Turtle()
for color in colors:
    t.color(color)
    t.goto(t.xcor() + 50, 0)

colors = ['red', 'blue', 'green', 'orange', 'purple']
turtles = [turtle.Turtle() for _ in range(5)]
for t in turtles:
    t.color(colors)
    t.goto(turtles.index(t) * 50, 0)

questions.explanationHtml: To assign unique properties (like color and position) to objects during creation, you need to iterate with an index. enumerate is perfect for getting both the index and the item from a list. The index can then be used to calculate a unique position, like i * 50. The item (color) can be used to set the turtle's color. Using a loop with range(len(colors)) and then indexing colors[i] also works correctly.

questions.promptHtml: Which Python keyword is used to create a loop that continues as long as a condition is true?
questions.explanationHtml: The while keyword in Python is used to execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a given boolean condition evaluates to True.

questions.promptHtml: Imagine a game where a player and a bot start with 100 health points each. Which while loop condition correctly ensures the game continues as long as both are alive (health > 0)?
questions.explanationHtml: The loop should continue only when both player_health is greater than 0 AND bot_health is greater than 0. The and operator ensures both conditions must be true for the loop to run.

questions.promptHtml: What is the output of the following code snippet?

choices.html: The code will produce a syntax error.

questions.explanationHtml: The expression (10 > 5 and 3 != 3) evaluates as follows: 10 > 5 is True. 3 != 3 is False. True and False evaluates to False. Therefore, the else block is executed, printing 'Condition is False'.

questions.promptHtml: Given the code snippet from a game, user_move(user, bot) is executed before bot_move(user, bot). Which of the following statements are potential issues or consequences of this specific order?

choices.html: If the user's move wins the game, the bot might still get to perform a move before the loop condition is re-evaluated and terminates.
The code will crash if the user_move function reduces the bot's health to zero.
It's possible for the game to end in a draw, even if the user landed the winning blow first.
The while loop will not terminate correctly if the user wins.

questions.explanationHtml: If the user's move defeats the bot, the bot's health might become zero or less. The loop condition might not be checked again until after the bot_move function is called, allowing a 'defeated' bot to make a final move. This could lead to a draw if the bot's final move also defeats the user. The loop will correctly terminate, but potentially after an extra, unintended action has occurred.

questions.promptHtml: In Object-Oriented Programming, what is the term for a specific realization or a single occurrence of a class?
questions.explanationHtml: An object is an instance of a class. The class acts as a blueprint, and the object is the actual entity built from that blueprint, possessing the defined attributes and methods.

questions.promptHtml: Given the following code, how would you create an object (instance) of the Animal class?
questions.explanationHtml: To create an instance of a class, you call the class name followed by parentheses (), which invokes its constructor (__init__ method). This newly created object is then typically assigned to a variable.

questions.promptHtml: What is the expected output of the following Python code?
questions.explanationHtml: The isinstance() function checks if an object is an instance of a specified class. In this case, lion is an object created from the Animal class, so isinstance(lion, Animal) returns True.

questions.promptHtml: Which of the following statements are true regarding classes and objects in Python?

choices.html: A class is a blueprint used to create objects.
An object is considered an instance of a class.
An object and a class are interchangeable terms.
The __init__() method is called when an object is instantiated.

questions.explanationHtml: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. An object is an instance of a class. The __init__ method, also known as the constructor, is a special method that is automatically called when a new instance of a class is created.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used in Python to bring a module's functionality into your current program?
questions.explanationHtml: The import statement is used to make code in one module available in another. To use functions from the random module, you must first write import random.

questions.promptHtml:
Given the following code, what is the purpose of the random.choice(options) line?

import random

options = ['rock', 'paper', 'scissors']
computer_move = random.choice(options)
print(computer_move)

choices.html: It selects and returns a single random element from the options list.

questions.explanationHtml: The random.choice() function takes a sequence (like a list) as an argument and returns one of its items at random. In this case, it will select either 'rock', 'paper', or 'scissors' and assign it to the computer_move variable.

questions.promptHtml:
What will be the output of the following code snippet?

import random

class Player:
  def __init__(self, name):
    self.name = name

players = [Player("Alice"), Player("Bob")]
chosen_player = random.choice(players)
print("Winner is " + chosen_player.name)

choices.html: It will print the memory address of the chosen player object.
Either "Winner is Alice" or "Winner is Bob", depending on the random selection.

questions.explanationHtml: The code creates a list of two Player objects. random.choice(players) selects one of these objects randomly. The final line then accesses the .name attribute of the chosen object and concatenates it with the string. Since the choice is random, the output will be either "Winner is Alice" or "Winner is Bob".

questions.promptHtml:
Which of the following lines of code would be valid uses of functions from the random module, assuming import random has been run?

# Assume these variables are defined:
my_list = [10, 20, 30, 40]
my_tuple = ('a', 'b', 'c')
my_string = "hello"
questions.explanationHtml: random.choice() can operate on any non-empty sequence, including lists, tuples, and strings. It will return a single, randomly chosen element from the sequence. random.choices(my_list, k=2) is also valid and returns a list of k elements chosen from the population with replacement.

questions.promptHtml:
What will be printed to the console when the following code is executed?

def compare(a, b):
    if a > b:
        print("You win!")
    elif a == b:
        print("Tie")
    else:
        print("Computer wins!")

compare(5, 10)
questions.explanationHtml: The function compare is called with a=5 and b=10. The condition a > b (5 > 10) is false. The condition a == b (5 == 10) is also false. Therefore, the code inside the else block is executed, printing 'Computer wins!'.

questions.promptHtml: Which keyword is used to define a new function in Python?
questions.explanationHtml: In Python, the def keyword is used to start a function definition, followed by the function name, parameters in parentheses, and a colon.

questions.promptHtml:
Consider the following Python function:

def compare(a, b):
    if a > b:
        print("You win!")
    elif a == b:
        print("Tie")
    else:
        print("Computer wins!")

What is the output of compare('apple', 'banana')?

choices.html: It will raise a TypeError because you can't compare strings.

questions.explanationHtml: In Python, strings are compared lexicographically (alphabetically). Since 'apple' comes before 'banana' in alphabetical order, 'apple' is considered less than 'banana'. Therefore, a > b and a == b are both false, and the else block is executed, printing 'Computer wins!'.

questions.promptHtml: Why are functions useful in programming?

choices.html: They allow for code reuse, reducing redundancy.
They improve the readability and organization of code.
They are the only way to perform mathematical operations.
They help break down complex problems into smaller, manageable pieces.

questions.explanationHtml: Functions are a cornerstone of structured programming. They allow for code reuse, avoiding repetition (DRY principle).
